card-001: Thorn didn't fight dirty. She just never stopped until you wished she had.
card-002: Rafael sold names like other men sold groceries. Fresh daily, no returns.
card-003: Zhao heard the sirens before they left the station. The Reapers never got caught.
card-004: The Black Market doesn't have muscle. It has Sammy, which is the same thing.
card-005: Kim walked in with nothing and left with your watch, your money, and your respect.
card-006: Sonny patched up what the street broke. The crew kept moving because of him.
card-007: Flint didn't torch the building for fun. He torched it so no one else could have it.
card-008: They'd send three men to find Rocco. He'd walk past all three going home.
card-009: Fats knew which signature to forge, which cop to call, which name to drop. That's the job.
card-010: Engine always warm. Mateo was already in gear when you were still lacing your boots.
card-011: Lefty let you think you were winning right up until the moment you weren't.
card-012: Santos turned dead weight into walking money. Even a sucker had value somewhere.
card-013: They told Gino the other guy was bigger. He hit him anyway. Twice.
card-014: Chin didn't snitch for money. He did it because everyone's guilty of something.
card-015: Ken spotted trouble before trouble knew where it was going.
card-016: The Jade Dragon doesn't send warnings. It sends Wong.
card-017: Frank could swap a good hand for a better one and make you feel like you won.
card-018: Two-Tone kept the Snakes running even when the blood on the pavement was theirs.
card-019: Billy didn't need an excuse. He just needed a lighter and a reason to walk away.
card-020: You could feel where Gabriela had been. You never saw where she went.
card-021: Red cleaned the turf like it was never contested. The Snakes paid him well for that.
card-022: Mickey drove for anyone who paid and nobody who talked. That was the deal.
card-023: Luis waited for the first hit to land. Then he started counting.
card-024: Smoke moved product like she moved through crowds — without a trace.
card-025: Dusty threw the first punch and acted surprised there was a second.
card-026: Cinder whispered secrets to strangers. The right ones paid. The wrong ones disappeared.
card-027: Richie watched the whole neighborhood from one window and missed nothing.
card-028: Crow worked three affiliations and owed loyalty to none. The money was the loyalty.
card-029: Hustler Nguyen swapped a losing hand for a winning one and blamed luck. It wasn't luck.
card-030: Lin stitched up the wounded in the back of a bodega and asked for nothing. Almost nothing.
card-031: Ortega brought the fire. The Jade Dragon brought the insurance. Same difference.
card-032: Sofia hit and vanished. The Dead Rabbits called it a gift. Her targets called it haunting.
card-033: Fats Matsuda made the books balance and the problems disappear. In that order.
card-034: Grim kept the engine running even when the job went sideways. Especially then.
card-035: Lucky waited for the bleeding to start. Then he went to work.
card-036: Patch moved anything that wasn't nailed down. And he had a crowbar for the rest.
card-037: Lola didn't know restraint. The Diablos didn't ask her to learn.
card-038: Tak sold secrets to both sides and called it hedging. The Reapers called it useful.
card-039: Bobby saw it coming three moves out. The Syndicate kept him in cigarettes and confidence.
card-040: Dagger held grudges like debts — with interest, compounded daily.
card-041: Oscar always had a better card. He just needed you to show yours first.
card-042: Joey kept the Reapers on their feet when the street tried to put them down.
card-043: Lucky Ishida burned slow and calculated. The Syndicate paid for the precision.
card-044: Smoke was in the room before you locked it. Gone before you turned the lights on.
card-045: Two-Tone Liu didn't pick sides. He cleaned up after them. Los Diablos paid well for that.
card-046: Sato had the car before the call came in. He always did.
card-047: Hector waited for the wound to open. He'd been doing this long enough to be patient.
card-048: Lucia could move a hot piece faster than the heat could follow the paperwork.
card-049: Copper didn't negotiate. He demonstrated. The Dead Rabbits respected demonstration.
card-050: Akira knew your hand before you played it. That's what your rent was really paying for.
card-051: Paulie read rooftops like a map. The Black Market never lost a runner on his watch.
card-052: Blade Yamamoto didn't just fight rival crews. He made them regret existing.
card-053: Mateo smiled like a man who'd already won. Usually he had.
card-054: Kim Aguilar saw bodies as a logistics problem. The Diablos stayed alive because of that.
card-055: Rosa didn't ask why. She asked how much and where. The Devils pointed. She burned.
card-056: Javier moved through the freelance world without affiliation. Or footprints.
card-057: Isabella turned a Jade Dragon problem into a Jade Dragon advantage. Quietly.
card-058: Copper's car didn't idle. He was always in motion, always three blocks ahead.
card-059: Blade Aguilar didn't go for the kill. He went for the already-bleeding.
card-060: Ines turned an awkward asset into a transaction. Nothing sat still in her hands.
card-061: Jackie never learned to pull a punch. The Dead Rabbits never asked her to.
card-062: Marco listened to everything the Jade Dragon moved through him. And charged accordingly.
card-063: Sung knew whose footsteps meant trouble before the foot hit the ground.
card-064: The Iron Devils don't forgive debts. Chin collects them.
card-065: Marco wore a smile that cost you everything if you believed it.
card-066: Chan Liu kept the Syndicate standing when a lesser medic would've let it fall.
card-067: Red Chung didn't watch things burn. He made sure they stayed that way.
card-068: Isabella vanished after every hit. The Saints called it a gift. Rivals called it a nightmare.
card-069: Dao didn't change loyalties. He changed paperwork. Same result, cleaner hands.
card-070: Danny had the car around the corner before the shot was fired.
card-071: Nails sat on a stool and watched the mark sweat. She liked that part.
card-072: Scar moved things that made cops nervous. He moved them anyway.
card-073: Bao hit like the word regret was invented specifically for the man across from him.
card-074: Gino Ramos knew your biggest secret. He hadn't decided what to do with it yet.
card-075: Scar Mendoza saw it all from the rooftop. The Reapers paid for that view.
card-076: Whiskey Gutierrez didn't care about your affiliation. He cared about what you owed.
card-077: Ray smiled at the mark and swapped the hand before the mark finished smiling back.
card-078: Lin Ferraro kept Kings Row breathing when the block tried its hardest to choke it.
card-079: Scar Kwon lit it fast and walked slow. The Diablos liked the style.
card-080: Hector was already gone by the time Kings Row realized they'd been hit.
card-081: Shin Suzuki made problems into arrangements. The Syndicate paid for arrangements.
card-082: Louie moved the car before the job started and was three states calm after it ended.
card-083: Sal waited for the first crack in the armor. He'd been patient his whole career.
card-084: Rico Fats turned sacrifice into advantage. The Jade Dragon kept him employed.
card-085: Rocco Caruso hit harder than anyone expected and kept no one's colors but his own.
card-086: Sonny Takahashi sold your biggest secret to your worst enemy. Business, not personal.
card-087: Frank Yang read the whole board from under the radar. Nobody saw him. That was the point.
card-088: Grim Russo's grudges compounded interest. Jade Dragon rivals paid in installments.
card-089: Diego always had one card you didn't see coming. He kept that one in reserve.
card-090: Min had seen worse. Fixed worse. Charged accordingly and didn't apologize.
card-091: Kim Silva didn't ask permission. She lit the match and sent the invoice later.
card-092: Richie Tran hit and evaporated. The Snakes called it a specialty. Targets called it horror.
card-093: Joey Fuentes made the problem disappear. The Jade Dragon asked no follow-up questions.
card-094: Mikey was already moving when the plan was still being explained. First out, every time.
card-095: Ryu watched the damage stack up and started counting. Kings Row always did the math.
card-096: Valentina moved what couldn't be moved and sold what couldn't be sold. For the right price.
card-097: Santos hit like he had something to prove. He'd been proving it since he was nineteen.
card-098: Jackie knew your whole hand before you sat down. She charged the Devils for that.
card-099: Emilio read rooftops, exits, and faces. The Devils never lost a position he was watching.
card-100: Louie Enforcer didn't work the edges. He defined them. The Reapers paid him to.
weap-01: The Bowie has opened enough deals that men stopped arguing about the terms.
weap-02: A karambit doesn't ask. It hooks and holds. The blade does the negotiating.
weap-03: Small enough for a pocket. Long enough to end the conversation.
weap-04: A machete says what fists can't. Louder. More permanent.
weap-05: The cleaver has a straightforward philosophy: one side's all you need.
weap-06: A stiletto doesn't threaten. It concludes.
weap-07: Four inches of sharpened metal. All you need in a place that doesn't ask questions.
weap-08: Field-stripped and cleaned, not because it's pretty, but because a dull blade costs you.
weap-09: A straight razor closes fast and opens slow. It's been both many times.
weap-10: Pulled from a pocket. Used once. Back in the pocket. Nobody writes this down.
weap-11: Aluminum and American. Thirty-four ounces of argument nobody wins.
weap-12: Improvised. Unglamorous. Does more damage than the thing you bought legally.
weap-13: They look like gloves. By the time you realize they're not, you're already counting stars.
weap-14: Pulled from a construction site. Now it's construction of a different kind.
weap-15: One tap behind the ear. The rest is paperwork.
weap-16: Built for fixing things. The definition of 'things' has widened considerably.
weap-17: A pipe wrench opens pipes and closes arguments. It's versatile.
weap-18: Someone asked why the nails. The bat answered for itself.
weap-19: Opens doors, breaks locks, redistributes the concept of ownership. Standard kit.
weap-20: Never goes back in the trunk. Too useful for that.
weap-21: Thermite doesn't care about walls. Neither does the crew that uses it.
weap-22: A bottle of intent. Lit when the argument is already over.
weap-23: Loud enough to matter. Cheap enough not to think twice.
weap-24: Three seconds of nothing to see. What happens in those three seconds is up to you.
weap-25: The can was innocent once. It has since learned what it was capable of.
weap-26: Homemade. The kind of weapon that says the owner has patience and resentment.
weap-27: Nothing to see. That's the whole point. Walk through it, walk away clean.
weap-28: You don't hear it. You don't see anything after. That's the design brief.
weap-29: Built to scatter. Effective enough that nobody asked for a receipt.
weap-30: One stick. One fuse. One less problem. The math is simple.
weap-31: Technically legal. Contextually, that's nobody's concern.
weap-32: The knife doesn't need to be close to make a point. That's the whole advantage.
weap-33: Entry-level. Still leaves a mark. Still leaves you wondering what was behind you.
weap-34: The dart is quiet. The paralytic is quieter. Nobody hears the deal fall through.
weap-35: A child's toy that grew up hard and decided on a different career path.
weap-36: One shot. Improvised. Enough. The zip gun doesn't apologize for its limitations.
weap-37: Industrial. Repurposed. Still puts holes in things that didn't ask to have holes.
weap-38: Silent as a suggestion. Effective as a statement. Nobody sees the blowgun.
weap-39: Walks through security. Borrowed from the job site. Returned to the street.
weap-40: Elastic, a stone, and a grievance. Three things that never go out of style.
weap-41: Quiet enough to use in a hotel lobby. That's not a hypothetical.
weap-42: Twelve inches of wire that was a lamp cord two hours ago. History is full of transitions.
weap-43: Sweet-smelling and permanent. The mark didn't see it coming because they couldn't.
weap-44: Looks like a fashion accessory. Functions like a final word.
weap-45: What's in it matters. How you get close enough to use it matters more.
weap-46: A wire saw has no opinions. It simply takes direction very seriously.
weap-47: Fifty cents from the dry cleaner. Unlimited applications, if you're patient.
weap-48: One drop on the right surface. The rest is chemistry the body didn't consent to.
weap-49: Bar ice. Delivered directly. The last thing the mark asked for.
weap-50: The voltage is temporary. The lesson tends to stick.
drug-01: Hyper Snap wires you tight and turns the next three minutes into a career.
drug-02: Nitro Blast doesn't feel like much until you're already moving too fast to stop.
drug-03: Sonic Crack. One line, one second, one version of yourself you didn't know existed.
drug-04: Flash Rush makes you faster than your judgment. That's the whole risk and the whole appeal.
drug-05: Blitz Charge doesn't warn you when it wears off. It just does. Suddenly.
drug-06: Turbo Spike hits the nerves first. The confidence comes after and costs more.
drug-07: Bolt Kick is what the corner sells when it wants to keep you coming back. You come back.
drug-08: Rapid Zing burns clean on the way in and dirty on the way out. Three-hour window.
drug-09: Surge Buzz. Cheaper than sleep, faster than food, shorter than either.
drug-10: Lightning Zip. By the time you felt it, you'd already made three decisions you'll regret.
drug-11: Slow Fog doesn't erase you. It just puts a thick glass between you and everything that hurts.
drug-12: Dreamy Lull. The body stops fighting back. That's either the point or the problem.
drug-13: Still Sleep asks nothing of you. That's the offer. That's why you take it.
drug-14: Midnight Mist. It won't make you forget everything. Just the things you couldn't afford.
drug-15: Quiet Peace was named by someone who'd never actually had either. Close enough.
drug-16: Drowsy Rest puts you somewhere soft and takes the corner's edge with it. Temporarily.
drug-17: Mellow Dusk holds the bad hour at arm's length. The hour comes back. It always comes back.
drug-18: Heavy Haze. The weight lifts and the noise goes quiet and then you're somewhere else.
drug-19: Hush Cloud moves through you like weather. Calm, total, and brief.
drug-20: Deep Shade. The corner calls it medicine. The clinic calls it something else.
drug-21: Spiral Pulse folds the room in half and puts you inside. The opponent isn't sure what they're seeing.
drug-22: Warped Vision. You see enemies where there are none. They see you coming when you're already there.
drug-23: Aurora Trip starts beautiful. The paranoia comes with the second hour.
drug-24: Prismatic Echo makes every sound a location you can't trust. That's the corner's weapon.
drug-25: Phantom Bloom. You're not sure what you saw. That's the product's best feature.
drug-26: Kaleidoscope Wave. By the third hit, reality is advisory. The street knows this.
drug-27: Vivid Flux. Clarity and chaos, back and forth, no schedule. The dose decides.
drug-28: Neon Lens. Everything glows. Nothing connects. The night becomes a series of wrong conclusions.
drug-29: Fractal Prism. Each dose splits the next decision into more pieces than you can hold.
drug-30: Crystal Dream. Crystalline and cold and wrong in ways you only understand afterward.
drug-31: Savage Mass. Vanity at first. Then necessity. The line between them disappeared somewhere.
drug-32: Gorilla Pump. The veins surface. The weight doubles. The mind narrows. That's the trade.
drug-33: Iron Fury. What the gym doesn't give you, the needle does. The street fills the gap.
drug-34: Titan Power. The body grows to fill the ambition. The ambition grows to fill the body.
drug-35: Tank Slam. One hit and the pain stops registering. That's either progress or a warning.
drug-36: Mammoth Bulk. The body becomes a wall. The mind becomes an afterthought.
drug-37: Bull Rage. The anger was already there. This just gives it somewhere to go.
drug-38: Brute Surge. Strength borrowed from tomorrow, paid back with interest on Monday.
drug-39: Raw Crush. Nothing subtle about it. That's the only honest thing the street sells.
drug-40: Heavy Might. The muscles remember this one long after the brain wishes they hadn't.
drug-41: Numb Fix. The pain becomes theoretical. You can still hit. You stop worrying about being hit.
drug-42: Dead Drop. One hit and the body stops keeping score. Dangerous way to play the game.
drug-43: Cold Hit. Ice water in the vein and then three seconds of absolutely nothing. Then the work.
drug-44: Ghost Haze. The corner's bargain: presence without feeling. You pay the difference later.
drug-45: Blank Out. Takes what hurts and makes it someone else's problem. Yours catches up eventually.
drug-46: Faded Smoke. The bruises don't register until the high clears. By then it doesn't matter.
drug-47: Void End. Empties the clock so you can fill it with something you can actually finish.
drug-48: Black Burn. It goes down hard and keeps you standing when standing costs everything.
drug-49: Shadow Fade. The corner sells it as numbness. The body pays for it as damage deferred.
drug-50: Dark Nod. One more. Just one more. The corner counts on the fact that you mean it.
currency-100: A hundred buys silence for a night. Morning costs more.
currency-1000: A grand changes the story. Not always to yours.
mythic-01: The second blade was already moving before the first one landed.
mythic-02: The ledger doesn't lie. The Accountant just decides when you get to see it.
mythic-03: A block without a blueprint is a battlefield. The Architect prefers blueprints.
mythic-04: The Informer already knew what you were carrying. The question was the price.
mythic-05: You retreated to be safe. The Ghost was already waiting where safe used to be.
mythic-06: Three before breakfast. Four if the coffee took too long.
mythic-07: No colors. No grudges. No affiliation survives the Fixer's particular brand of neutral.
mythic-08: The Magistrate doesn't fear raids. The Magistrate invented due process. For himself.
mythic-09: You know you saw something. The Phantom knows you can't prove it.
mythic-10: The Reaper doesn't leave glancing blows. Every hit is a bill that comes due.
